questions.text: I believe that treating what people say as what they mean is a form of respect.
I hold the view that the ability to remain emotionally steady during distressing events is a competence that every serious professional should aspire to develop.
I notice that my breathing changes dramatically when I'm emotionally activated.
I prefer to have all the information before starting a task.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering has given me a clarity of purpose that has shaped the direction of my life more than any ambition or goal.
I believe the measure of my day is how much I gave, not how much I gained.
I tend to choose opportunities that advance my personal career over those that primarily benefit others.
I throw things together at the last minute and it usually works out fine.
I am energized by having the final say.
I experience nostalgia as a deep, aching longing rather than a gentle wistfulness.
I sense when rapport between two people is genuine versus performative.
I notice when someone's laugh does not quite reach their eyes.
I feel that keeping score of what I've given defeats the purpose of giving.
I find that I often refine and build on ideas others have introduced.
I experience anticipation as a mild interest rather than an overwhelming buzz.
I believe that the moral priority of suffering is the criterion by which I evaluate whether a society or institution is truly just.
I think of my future self as someone I am actively building conditions for.
I enjoy the challenge of persuading others to follow my direction.
